context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection on informal settlements and illegality. I’m kinda stuck on how to connect informalization, the formal/informal economy, and whether formalization is actually the answer.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Let's dig in. Before we talk structure or connections, tell me: in your own words, why do you think someone ends up living in an informal settlement in the first place? What forces or circumstances might push them there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting out and needs to be oriented toward the problem without being given any content or structure. I'll use a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about what they already know, specifically about how informalization happens, which is the first part of the prompt. This avoids scaffolding and lets me assess their baseline understanding.

exchanges.student: I think it’s mostly bc formal housing is too expensive or unavailable, and ppl move to cities faster than the state can plan for. Also maybe weak land laws or exclusion make illegality kinda the only option?
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts — you've identified some key drivers. Now think about this: once people are living and working informally, do you think the informal and formal economies are completely separate from each other, or do they interact? If they interact, who benefits from that relationship?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a solid foundational answer touching on affordability, rural-urban migration, state capacity, and exclusion. I should affirm what's strong without over-praising, then push them to go deeper on the relationship between the informal and formal economy, which is the next part of the prompt. I'll ask a Socratic question to get them thinking about how the two economies relate rather than exist in isolation.

exchanges.student: I don’t think they’re separate tbh, but I’m not sure how to say it clearly. Like informal workers still supply labor/services to the formal economy, so maybe formal firms and the state both benefit sometimes?
exchanges.tutor: You're onto something important there. Now flip it around — what does the informal worker or settler get out of this relationship? And does the benefit flow equally in both directions, or is there an imbalance of power at play?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with the idea of interdependence and who benefits. I should affirm this without giving away more content, and push them to think about the flip side — what does the informal sector get out of this relationship, and is it a fair exchange? This moves them toward a more nuanced understanding of power dynamics, which is central to the course themes.

exchanges.student: I guess informal workers get income, housing access, and a way to survive rn. But nah it doesn’t feel equal—formal actors prob get cheap labor while informal people take more risk and have less protection.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — the risk and reward are unevenly distributed. Now here's a trickier question: in the absence of formal state planning, who do you think actually shapes outcomes in informal settlements? Who makes the rules, allocates space, or organizes economic activity there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a strong understanding of the power asymmetry in the informal-formal relationship. Now I should move them toward the third part of the prompt — who plans for outcomes in informal economies — since they've been dwelling on the relationship between the two economies. This is a key and often overlooked question that will enrich their reflection.

exchanges.student: Maybe local brokers, landlords, political patrons, NGOs, gangs sometimes, and residents too? I’m a little lost on how to group them tho.
exchanges.tutor: You've listed a diverse set of actors. Rather than grouping them, think about this: what do some of them have that others don't, in terms of power or legitimacy? How does that affect whose 'plan' actually wins out in an informal settlement?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a good range of actors. Rather than helping them categorize these actors (which could lead to me doing the intellectual work for them), I should push them to think about what these actors have in common or what distinguishes them — specifically around legitimacy and power, which are core course themes. This will help them develop their own analytical framework rather than relying on mine.

exchanges.student: I think the ppl w/ coercion, money, or ties to the state matter most, even if they’re not legit officially. Is the idea basically that planning still happens, just through messy unofficial power?
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation — planning without official legitimacy is still planning. Now, given everything you've said about how informality works and who benefits, what do you think happens when the state steps in and tries to formalize things — like giving people land titles? Does that fix the underlying problems you've described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely insightful point about planning happening through unofficial power structures. This is a strong conceptual move. Now is the right time to pivot to the final and most contested part of the prompt — whether formalization is the answer — since they've built enough understanding of the informal dynamics to critically evaluate that question.

exchanges.student: I don’t think it fully fixes it tbh. Titles might help security, but if poverty, uneven power, and lack of services stay the same, formalization seems kinda partial.
exchanges.tutor: That's a well-reasoned position — and it's actually at the heart of a lot of debates in development planning. You've now worked through all four parts of the prompt. I think you have enough material to start a first draft. What would your opening argument or framing sentence look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reached a nuanced critical stance on formalization, which is the core tension the prompt wants them to grapple with. They've now covered all four parts of the prompt in conversation. Since we've had about 6 exchanges and they have solid raw material, it's time to push them toward integrating everything into a written attempt. I should affirm their thinking briefly and redirect toward writing.

exchanges.student: Maybe: informal settlements aren’t just signs of state failure, but outcomes of exclusion, uneven development, and linked formal-informal systems. Does that sound too broad?
exchanges.tutor: Instead of me judging it, ask yourself: does that sentence make a specific enough claim that a reader knows where your argument is going? And does it set up the four questions from the prompt — informalization, the formal-informal relationship, who plans, and whether formalization works?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate their opening sentence, which risks me doing their writing work. I should not polish or suggest language, but I can push them to assess it themselves by asking what specific claim it makes and whether it sets up the four parts of the prompt. This keeps them in the driver's seat while moving toward writing.

exchanges.student: yeah kinda, but it prob needs the last part on formalization more clearly. Maybe I should add that formalization alone isn’t enough?
exchanges.tutor: Good self-correction. You've done the thinking — now it's time to put it on paper. Try writing your opening paragraph based on what you just said, and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is self-correcting and thinking critically about their own framing, which is excellent. I should affirm this briefly and push them to now actually write rather than continuing to plan in the abstract. We're at exchange 9 and they have all the pieces — it's time to move them toward producing the draft.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think my opening should say informal settlements come from exclusion and survival strategies, that formal and informal systems depend on each other, and that formalization alone doesn’t solve the deeper issues. Can u help me turn that into 2–3 opening sentences?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I can do — writing the sentences is your job, not mine. You just described your three main ideas clearly in plain language. That's actually a great starting point. Try writing it out yourself, even roughly, and we can talk about the ideas in it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to write for them, which I must refuse as per the rules. I should be clear and firm about this boundary while redirecting them to do the writing themselves. They have all the ideas — they just need to commit to putting them into words.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently using probing questions to guide the student toward their own conclusions rather than providing answers. The tutor never gave away key ideas, maintained appropriate scaffolding by building on each student response, kept responses concise and focused, and firmly declined to write sentences for the student in the final turn. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Each turn posed a question that advanced the student's thinking. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., 'Good instincts,' 'That's a sharp observation') and used diagnostic questioning before advancing. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, notably in Turn 8 redirecting the student to self-evaluate their thesis against the prompt's four questions rather than just validating it. 2.1: The conversation progressed steadily through all four prompt questions without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The tutor kept the conversation anchored to the assignment throughout. 3.1: Responses were consistently brief and interactive. 3.2: Tone was supportive and coaching-oriented, with no grading language.
